excerpt_en: Last day in Madrid! I had nine shoots scheduled from 9 AM to 5 PM, but in Spain some people don’t reply to confirmations and some cancel last minute. I sent a message asking anyone who would be late to contact me, and none of the participants were late. First was Rebecca, an English teacher from Manchester now in Madrid. We even connected with FOMUS members in Sagishima over video; she was happy to talk and wants to meet when she goes to Japan. Then I shot Maya at Madrid Rio Park, followed by a pro model Natalie at Plaza Mayor (she was an accessory model, not just a Masu Photo model). Last was 20-year-old Jade with her family, and she posed well after reviewing my Masu Photo materials. I reflected on how far Masu Photo has come and that longer stays are needed to build real connections. Next stop: Ireland for about a year.
body_en:
Last day of activities in Madrid!

Since it’s Saturday, maybe everyone is off,

and Masu Photo bookings came in all at once.

Originally, I had 9 shoots scheduled.

From 9 AM to 5 PM.

But this is Spain.

Even when we make plans,

some people don’t respond at the day-before confirmation stage,

and I assume they probably won’t come.

In the end, people who come, come, and those who don’t, don’t.

Some even say,

“Actually, I can’t make it”

only after the time arrives.

Is this a cultural difference??

Still, the day before,

I sent a message saying,

“If you’re going to be late, please contact me.”

So none of the participants were late!!

*It’s important to make the point clear!

*I’m Japanese

I don’t think being late is bad.

I just dislike people who are late without contacting me. Lol

Even for people who might be loose with time,

just sending this one message makes many show up on time.

The first shoot was with Rebecca, from Manchester,

now living in Madrid, working as a freelance English teacher.

As expected of an English teacher,

her English was super easy to understand!

She moves her base every six months to a year

and teaches English there.

She even lived in Japan for about half a year and studied at Keio University!

I think that lifestyle sounds fun too.

Changing locations every six months while working could be nice.

It was a shoot full of learning!

After the shoot,

I had a short video call with FOMUS members currently doing container DIY on Sagishima!

Rebecca had lived in Japan for six months,

so she speaks a little Japanese,

and I wanted the Spanish Masu model to connect with Japanese FOMUS members,

so I quickly contacted Kuma and connected them. ✨

Today, Kuma, Yuri, and Yusaku were working on Sagishima,

and I wanted to express my gratitude in my own words,

so the timing was perfect.

*After the shoot, Rebecca messaged me,

“I’m glad I could talk with FOMUS members,”

“I want to meet when I go to Japan,”

she wrote.

I was glad I connected them.

And!

The next shoot was in the same place!

By the way, the location was

a park called Madrid Rio.

It’s about 20 minutes from my current lodging,

and more than 40 minutes from the Couchsurfing place,

so looking back, changing accommodations was the right call!

The second person was Maya.

She felt similar to me in vibe.

Relaxed, and then quick when it’s time to work.

She was really interested in FOMUS!

The shoot went so smoothly

that we mostly just talked.

She was probably a little older than me,

and her words and actions felt very mature,

so I had a great time. Lol

Now, we changed locations

and went to Plaza Mayor!

The third person was Natalie, a professional model.

She wasn’t found via FOMUS ads —

I searched and directly offered her.

Honestly, pulling in pros is tough.

I reach out to 50 people,

and if 5 reply, that’s good.

*Even then, schedule matching is another story

She had great energy,

and since she’s a pro,

the shoot moved at crazy speed. Lol

She wasn’t a Masu Photo model —

I hired her as an accessory model.

That’s a big difference.

Pros as accessory models,

amateurs as Masu Photo models —

that strategy lets both wear masu

and hold it too.

Finally, the fourth person was

20-year-old Jade.

She came with her family.

She had never modeled before, but

she read through the FOMUS account and the Masu Photo booklet I sent,

and posed really well.

I usually don’t give directions.

Because if I direct,

it becomes my art.

This is a collaboration,

and only when their poses

and my shooting come together

does it work.

That’s the fun —

maybe that’s why we don’t call selfies “Masu Photo.”

We had fun conversations the whole time!

After Masu Photo in Madrid

Looking back,

with support from everyone,

I came overseas alone,

and three years ago, in Japan,

when I asked “Want to take a photo holding a masu?”

90% ignored me.

Now I get offers from America and Spain,

and people overseas find FOMUS projects interesting.

Being able to confirm that makes me feel it was worth it.

Still, in this short time,

I only shot with a tiny handful of people.

To expand the FOMUS brand,

I need both the grass-roots approach of persuading one by one,

and connections with models or business owners who have strong influence.

That can’t be done in just a week,

so like the first person Rebecca,

I feel I need to stay at least six months in a country to really take root.

From tomorrow, it’s finally Ireland, the main destination.

Ireland is planned for about a year,

so I’ll approach it completely differently from America and Spain.

If anything, it’ll be closer to how I did the Philippines.

These past five weeks were full of new experiences every day.

Ireland is unknown territory for me too.

I want to enjoy that as well!!

PS

Last Spain meal: paella!!

Two servings were easy to eat. 😂

excerpt_en: After about 40 days in the U.S. and Spain, my main journey in Ireland begins. It was originally fixed for July 28, and later I added the U.S. and Spain schedule, which ended up being 40 days to match Shimizu’s Peru challenge return. In those 40 days I had countless experiences — meeting people, seeing world-class entertainment, staying in locals’ homes, missing a flight, taking a night bus, and even being told it was uncomfortable that I wore clothes. I’ll base in Ireland for about a year to expand FOMUS, traveling around Europe too. I’ll attend a language school for three main reasons: improve English, build European connections, and learn culture through homestay. There are many Irish elements (Guinness, whiskey, folk music) I want to combine with FOMUS. I wrote this on the flight from Madrid to Dublin, with a mix of excitement and anxiety.
body_en:
So,

after about 40 days of the U.S. and Spain expedition,

my main activities in Ireland begin.

Originally, going to Ireland on July 28 was fixed,

and I added the U.S. and Spain schedule after that, which was the right order.

At first, I was thinking about 20 days, but

by matching the return date of GPS runner Shimizu’s Peru challenge,

it ended up being 40 days.

Every day brought new discoveries,

so in a way, it might have been inevitable.

It was only 40 days, but

so much happened.

So many encounters,

experiencing world-class entertainment,

staying in locals’ homes,

good and bad moments,

missing a flight,

taking an overnight bus,

being told “it’s uncomfortable that you’re wearing clothes,”

and experiences I could never have in normal life in Japan.

For me, this experience is an asset,

and I wrote these articles so I wouldn’t forget,

but by sharing them with you,

I hope you find today’s overseas reality in 2024

and my activities interesting.

Now, from here, I’m going to Ireland 🇮🇪.

To expand the masu brand “FOMUS,”

I’ll base myself in Ireland for about a year.

That said,

I won’t stay only in Ireland —

I’ll travel around Europe while working.

From Ireland, it’s much faster

to reach other European countries than from Japan.

From August to October,

I’ll mostly be active in Ireland.

In September, only for the Medoc Marathon period,

I’ll move to Bordeaux.

As for basic activities,

the first three months are about

building connections with people in Ireland.

I’m actually going to a language school in Ireland.

There are a few reasons.

1. Strengthen English ability

First, improving English.

Ireland is English-speaking,

but apparently has some accent,

so if I can learn to understand it well here,

I believe it will work in other countries too.

Also, to work globally, “English”

is absolutely required for communication right now,

so I’ll master it by the time I’m 30.

Then after 30, I’ll use English

to expand business overseas.

2. Build European connections

Language schools bring people

from all over Europe.

That means by building connections there,

it becomes easier to create footholds

for other countries after October.

Honestly, the three months in the Philippines

helped expand the FOMUS brand there,

and I also built horizontal connections within the school.

They still follow my SNS and

we sometimes keep in touch.

When I go to Southeast Asia next year,

I could meet up with them,

and when they come to Japan,

I want to create opportunities forexchange using FOMUS.

So in these three months,

it’s important that people at the same school think,

“It’s fun to be with Massu.”

That leads to the next stage.

3. Learn culture through homestay

For the next two months,

I’ll do homestay in Dublin.

By attending the language school,

I could choose homestay or a dorm,

and I chose homestay.

Because connecting with locals

is the most important thing.

Some English ability is required, but

this communication is what will help FOMUS and me

take root in Ireland.

There are other reasons too, but

I’ll attend a language school in Ireland.

Ireland has

Guinness beer,

Irish whiskey,

Irish folk songs,

and lots of content I want to combine with FOMUS.

Especially Irish music,

I plan to use it for FOMUS events and also for the KUKU character’s music,

so I’ll find famous songs with expired copyrights!

And since Ireland is probably a village society too,

I want to be accepted as a villager

and expand FOMUS through various collaborations.

That’s the plan for Ireland.

This is the main part!! Lol

Honestly, many unexpected things will happen.

No one knows if spending the last years of my 20s

on Ireland activities is the right choice.

Still, in my 30s,

I want to fully use the skills I built in my 20s,

so I’ll give everything I have now.

And I’ll link it well

so it positively affects the projects in Japan

that involve FOMUS too.

So,

I wrote these thoughts on the flight from Madrid to Dublin.

Right now I’m a mix of excitement and anxiety.

I’m going to enjoy even that!!

Thank you so much for reading this far!!

excerpt_en: It’s November. I’m Massu, running the masu brand FOMUS and working as a photographer. I’ve been based in Ireland since late July to bring FOMUS to Europe, but after three months I still haven’t made a big breakthrough. I’ve been moving nonstop — launching a meetup group in Dublin and running walking and card-game events. Now that I’m free in the mornings after language school, I noticed people in Dublin drink from midday, especially on Fridays, so I tried a daytime pub-drinking event. I expected 5–8 participants; five came, and they were from six countries (Canada, Italy, Korea, France, Brazil, Japan). I provided nuts using a FOMUS masu. I mostly listened instead of moderating, but the conversation felt dull, so I think enforcing one-on-one talk rotations and setting topics would improve it. Day drinking also made people sleepy because of the food + alcohol combo. Still, experimenting reveals new insights, and I’ll refine it. Tomorrow is my first walking event in two weeks.
body_en:
It’s November now!

Since some people may be starting here,

let me introduce myself briefly.

I’m Massu!

I run the Japanese traditional craft masu brand “FOMUS”

and work as a photographer traveling the world.

To bring FOMUS to the world (Europe),

I’ve been based in Ireland since late July,

trying every possible approach.

And the reality is that things are not going

as I imagined.

Three months have passed,

but unfortunately,

I still haven’t made any big moves to expand FOMUS.

However, in these three months, I’ve been constantly moving,

and in Dublin, the capital of Ireland,

I launched an international exchange meetup group,

running walking and card game events.

Besides that, if there’s an interesting invitation,

I join everything — my footwork is extremely light.

This year, I’m based in Ireland,

traveling around Europe,

looking for clues to expand the FOMUS brand.

So,

if you want to know from the beginning,

please start with “Ireland Activity Log ①”

for a full picture!

With that said,

I’ll write today’s activity log.

🔻You never know until you try

For my first 12 weeks in Ireland,

I attended a language school in Dublin.

Weekdays were classes from 9 AM to 1 PM,

then I focused on FOMUS activities, but

now I’ve graduated,

so my mornings are free.

By being in Dublin every day,

I realized:

People drink from midday!!

Especially on Fridays,

they’re drinking hard from daytime!

Seeing this, I thought pub events are usually at night,

so I decided to try a “day drinking at a pub” event.

And today was the event!

Capacity was 15 people.

From my experience, 50%–70% usually no-show without notice,

so I predicted 5–8 people would come.

In the end, 5 people came!

And the interesting part is that

everyone was a different nationality.

Canada, Italy, Korea, France, Brazil,

and Japan.

Six countries gathered!

I’ve mostly been doing walking events,

so it’s been a while since I ran an event

where people just start talking in a group of first-time strangers.

Still, with drinks and snacks,

theexchange naturally heats up.

This time,

since I charged €5 per person,

I prepared nuts as a return,

and used a FOMUS masu as the nut bowl.

This also shows that it can be used in daily life.

As for theexchange,

this time I didn’t moderate and just listened.

The conversation still flowed, but

it wasn’t that interesting.

We started without any specific rules, but

I think we should add a rule of “you must talk one-on-one,”

even in this event.

Why? Because even with 6 people,

not everyone talked evenly, and each person’s conversation time shrank.

So even at the same table,

if we set a rule that enables one-on-one talking,

and rotate members by time,

satisfaction might be higher.

Personally, I can talk better one-on-one,

so regardless of a drinking event,

I want to try these measures

and see what results they bring.

There was also a downside of day drinking:

I assumed everyone would eat beforehand,

but they hadn’t, so they ordered chicken and potatoes at the pub.

That’s not a problem, but

daytime + food + alcohol is a triple combo,

and everyone got a bit sleepy.

So the sharpness of conversation

wasn’t as strong as during walking events.

The sleepiness happens because

they were listening more than talking,

and if we force one-on-one conversation,

we can probably solve that.

So setting talk themes may also help.

Anyway,

actually running an event reveals a lot.

I never expected the downside of day drinking

would be sleepiness. Lol

That’s why it leads to the question:

How do we address it?

Thinking about that and applying it next time —

creating this flow

is how I think my team and I will grow stronger.

So, after trying the day drinking event,

I had fun and found new challenges.

These are things only organizers

who constantly run events can see,

so it’s nice to have the freedom to do this.

As winter deepens,

walking events will probably drop in attendance.

That’s why I tried an indoor pub event.

Whether it works in the future depends on me,

so I want to build it with a long-term view.

In short,

trying the day drinking event showed me a lot.

Tomorrow is a walking event for the first time in about two weeks!

We have 70 reservations,

but I expect 10–20 participants.

I don’t know how it will actually turn out, but

if people who come get interested in me,

and connect to FOMUS, that would be the best!!

Let’s go!!

excerpt_en: It was Saturday, so I held my meetup group event in Ireland. I created the community to make friends, build deeper connections, and increase interest in me and FOMUS. The two main events are walking and card games. Today’s walking event had about 18 participants, mostly new, with many Japanese and French. The system is simple but structured: walk in pairs, change partners every 10 minutes (six rotations), then 자유exchange for an hour at a cafe. This one-on-one structure is very popular and prevents isolation. I always talk with everyone and collect feedback. After the walk, I hosted a SILVA card game event. SILVA is a FOMUS original game made in 2023; seeing it played by overseas participants proves it works internationally. The day ended with karaoke with the local Japanese community before I headed to the airport for an early flight.
body_en:
Since it’s Saturday,

I held my meetup group event in Ireland.

For details, see Ireland Activity Log ①, but in short,

I created a local community to make friends, build deep connections,

and increase people who are interested in me and FOMUS.

I mainly run two events:

walking and card games.

So, a walking event in the morning!

About 18 people joined today.

A few were repeaters,

but most were new.

Today there were many Japanese and French.

As expected, the common language is English.

Everyoneexchanges in English.

Small side note:

I’ve been planning and running events for about 5 years,

and I also join events run by others

if the organizer seems interesting, regardless of genre.

That’s why I understand both the organizer’s and participant’s mindset.

And if I can capture participants’latent needs, that’s perfect.

So, my walking event is very simple:

walk around the city for one hour while doing internationalexchange.

I always make people walk in two rows

and force one-on-one conversations.

I decide who talks with whom using special cards.

So you never know who you’ll talk to!

We rotate partners every 10 minutes,

and repeat that six times,

so you can talk with at least six people.

But with 18 people, only six?

That’s why after that, I provide one hour at a cafe

for freeexchange.

This two-layer structure of walking + cafe

creates closer relationships.

I always make sure to talk with everyone

and collect feedback, good or bad,

so I can fix things immediately.

The goal is for participants to feel

“I’m glad I joined! I want to join again!”

“I’ll go to Massu’s events.”

That kind of relationship can’t be built online alone.

Participants also find this walking system

very interesting.

Maybe not many people do this in the world.

It’s hard to challenge new things,

but it’s also extremely rewarding.

This time too,

many people said that

the one-on-one environment

is what they liked most.

When you have 3–4 people,

the number of conversation partners grows,

so someone always has to wait.

And if you can’t find someone to talk to,

you end up isolated, lowering satisfaction.

But in this walking event,

one-on-one conversations are guaranteed.

Instead of leaving “find someone to talk to”

to participants,

the organizer decides —

that approach worked very well.

Honestly, I’ve joined walking events in Japan too,

and while I naturally approach people,

some people find that hard and end up isolated.

Organizers then try to cover by approaching them,

but satisfaction still drops,

because isolation is possible.

Since I manage everything with number cards,

I don’t need to cover anyone.

This strategy really worked.

And once again, participants told me:

“Thanks for organizing such a lovely event.”

“I’ll join again!”

I’m truly grateful!

🔻Card game “SILVA” event

After the walking event,

participants went to lunch together,

so I’m glad I could play the connector role.

Wait, Massu didn’t go to lunch?

Right — because I had to host

the card game event from 1 PM,

so I couldn’t go.

The schedule was:

10:00 Walking event

11:30 Cafe

13:00 Card game SILVA event

The cafe time often goes until around 12:45,

so there was no time for lunch.

The card game was not regular playing cards,

but FOMUS’s original card game “SILVA.”

SILVA was created in 2023 with FOMUS members.

Who would have thought a year later

people overseas would be playing it... lol

SILVA includes effects that use FOMUS masu,

so I’d be happy if people bought the set together!

Here too, the common language is English.

Explaining the rules in English

gets easier after doing it for two months,

and participants catch on fast,

so the battles got intense. 🔥

At the same time,

I confirmed that SILVA works overseas.

That’s the biggest thing.

If the content was weak, there’d be no point,

but the quality is proven,

so I feel confident pushing it abroad more.

At some point,

I might bring SILVA to board game or card game shops too.

In the end,

we played from 1 PM to 3:30 PM!

In terms of internationalexchange,

should we talk more, or focus on the game?

This changes every time,

so I want to keep experimenting.

🔻End of couch-surfing stay

When I returned from Estonia last week,

I had already canceled my Ireland place,

so I had no home,

and stayed for a few days at Taiki’s house,

someone I’ve become close with here.

I’m going to Malta 🇲🇹 tomorrow,

and Taiki is moving this month,

so it was hard to keep my suitcase there.

So I arranged for another friend, Makoto, to store my suitcase!

My suitcase moves between friends’ houses,

while I travel overseas.

I’m just full of gratitude!!

And tonight,

I went to karaoke with core people in the Japanese community

plus their French and Italian friends.

I was invited, so I joined.

We enjoyed karaoke from 8 PM to 11 PM.

Then I left my suitcase with them,

and I went to Dublin airport.

My flight departs at 6:20 AM tomorrow,

so I went the night before.

Today, from morning to night,

I met people and connected all day.

It was extremely meaningful!

I made new overseas friends,

and I was so happy to join karaoke with

Japanese people who’ve lived in Dublin for a long time

(working locally for 2–8 years).

My life has changed massively in the past few years.

This is overwhelmingly due to “environment.”

For three years in Japan as an address hopper,

I was surrounded by amazing eccentrics,

and now in Ireland, I feel included in a community

of long-term Dublin residents with deep connections.

This accumulation will lead to FOMUS brand growth,

so I’ll keep acting more than anyone!!

Let’s go!!

excerpt_en: I relaxed at the Dublin lounge from 4 AM and flew at 6:30 to Malta 🇲🇹, sleeping during the flight. I hadn’t planned Malta, but a Ukrainian friend I met at my first Ireland meetup lives there and invited me. It was perfect timing since I had no place in Ireland. I reached my friend’s place by bus (one transfer, then a 20-minute walk because the bus was late), refreshed with a shower, and he cooked teriyaki chicken. He often hosts Japanese students and knows Japanese cooking. He then showed me around Sliema and St. Julian’s, with a great view, and we had dinner by the beach with Maltese beer. I was exhausted, so I slept before midnight. Malta chapter starts now.
body_en:
From 4 AM I relaxed at the Dublin lounge,

and took the 6:30 flight to Malta 🇲🇹!

I slept deeply during the flight.

*That was the only time I could sleep

🔻Why did I come to Malta?

I didn’t originally plan to go to Malta, but

a Ukrainian friend I met at my first meetup in Ireland

lives in Malta, and told me,

“If you come, stay with me!”

So I actually came. 😁

Also,

it was perfect timing since I had no place in Ireland.

*If I had rented a place, I’d still have to pay when I’m away

So I came to Malta at this timing.

🔻Another couch stay! Lol

After arriving at the airport, I went straight to my friend’s house.

There was a transfer, but

the first bus was from the airport,

so it was easy, but

the connecting bus didn’t come on time,

so I walked about 20 minutes

with heavy luggage.

I’m used to it now, so no problem!

Then I arrived and we reunited!

He’s about 10 years older,

but I’m staying anyway. Lol

I arrived,

took a shower right away and refreshed!

While I did that,

he made teriyaki chicken.

He often invites Japanese students

and cooks Japanese food,

so he knows a lot.

After that,

he showed me around Malta.

His home is in Sliema,

so we walked about 20 minutes to St. Julian’s.

He took me to a spot with a full view of Malta.

Then we had dinner near the beach.

I drank Maltese beer too.

Since I was sleep-deprived,

I went to bed before midnight.

So,

this is the start of the Malta chapter!

What kind of encounters await?

I’m excited!

excerpt_en: Day 2 in Malta. I had a Masu Photo shoot in the capital Valletta, so I took a ferry. It was crowded even on a weekday and I waited about 45 minutes, but made it on time. The model was a Colombian living in Malta and working as a casino dealer. After the shoot I explored Valletta — I liked the streets — and ate gelato (the pistachio shop I wanted was sold out). I visited St. John’s Co-Cathedral (15 euros), which was amazing. In the evening, a Japanese-cooking event got cancelled, so we cooked at home and went to a Malta international meetup (game night). I met many people, mostly Brazilian and Colombian, and introduced masu. After returning home at 11, I ended up in a 3-hour late-night love-talk in English with my Ukrainian friend.
body_en:
Day 2 in Malta.

Right away, I had a Masu Photo shoot

in the capital Valletta,

so I took a ferry.

Even though it was a weekday,

it was super crowded,

so I couldn’t get on the first ferry

and had to wait about 45 minutes...

Still, I made it to the meeting time.

This time, the model was a Colombian living in Malta.

She works as a casino dealer here.

It was my first time in Valletta too,

so it was shooting + sightseeing.

In Japan, I usually shoot with people who’ve lived locally for a long time,

so they guide me to tourist spots,

but overseas, people come from everywhere,

so it doesn’t work like that. Lol

Still, the shoot went very smoothly!

After the shoot, we parted,

and I explored Valletta alone.

I personally liked Valletta’s streets.

I walked around,

and went to a gelato shop instead of lunch.

The pistachio flavor at the shop I wanted was already sold out,

so I went to another gelato place.

There wasn’t much pistachio flavor,

so it was a bit disappointing.

I still haven’t found anything that beats the gelato I had in Venice as a student. Lol

🔻To the cathedral

Then I went to St. John’s Co-Cathedral,

which is a must-see in Valletta.

It’s 15 euros — a bit expensive —

but you should see it.

Look up the details yourself. Lol

Anyway, it was amazing!

🔻To a Malta meetup

Originally, at 17:00,

there was supposed to be a Japanese cooking event with a Japanese woman invited by my Ukrainian friend,

but she canceled last minute, so the event didn’t happen.

So, after cooking dinner ourselves,

we went to a Malta international meetup.

Monday was game night,

but lots of people were just talking too.

So Iexchangeed with many people.

There were a lot of Brazilians and Colombians.

My goal is to let as many people as possible know about FOMUS,

so I casually talked about masu here and there.

I always carry a masu,

so this is a strong point.

Then we returned home around 11, and

for some reason, the two of us talked about love until 3 AM. Lol

I don’t find that topic very interesting, but

I treated it as English practice,

and communicated in English for about 3 hours.

His second language is English too,

so it was easy for me to understand — very helpful!

So,

day 2 in Malta was very meaningful!

excerpt_en: A Masu Photo shoot was canceled because the model wasn’t feeling well, so I used the free time to visit Mdina, an old city about an hour away by bus. I chose it because it could inspire the world of the FOMUS character KUKU, which has a two-layer world (ground and underground). Mdina and nearby Rabat have caves and high walls that fit that vibe. The vines on walls were especially inspiring. I also visited an underground church cave in Rabat. For lunch I tried a cannolo, but it wasn’t that great. In the evening I had dinner with two Japanese people working in Malta — connections through a friend’s niece. They told me about life in Malta, the small Japanese community, and potential opportunities. Then I went to a Tuesday night drinking meetup, chatted about BABYMETAL with a Chinese man, and realized I still need practice ending long conversations.
body_en:
I had one Masu Photo shoot planned,

but the model wasn’t feeling well, so it was canceled.

That left me with a free daytime schedule, so I decided to go somewhere.

After researching, I found Mdina, a place with old streets,

and took a one-hour bus ride there!

I chose this town because

it seemed like it could inspire the world of the character “KUKU,”

one of the FOMUS projects.

KUKU’s world has two layers,

the surface and the underground, where everyone lives,

and around this town there are cave-like underground spaces

and high walls, so I thought I should visit it in person.

So, the research begins!

Same country, but

it felt different from Valletta,

and it was fun to walk around!

What especially inspired me for the KUKU world

was the vines covering the walls.

In KUKU’s world, the underground of “Tree Village”

has a setting where “Kukunochi” appeared long ago and created trees,

so it needs traces of greenery spreading across a once-barren place.

Scenes like these etched into my memory.

Ideas are combinations of past experiences;

you can’t create ideas from zero.

Everything is experience, so

learning “this kind of method exists” was valuable for me.

If I gain many experiences while I’m young,

it raises the chance they’ll help later.

For now, I invest in experience.

Because I invest in experience,

I already did pure travel in college,

so now I combine it with business

to maximize each experience.

That’s a side note, but

Mdina was a great place!

Then I walked about 15 minutes to Rabat.

There are multiple churches here,

and one of them has a huge underground cave.

Apparently they did missionary work underground

to spread Catholicism.

It looks like a normal church,

but there’s an entrance underground.

A huge underground space spreads out.

This matched KUKU’s world perfectly,

so I learned a lot, and also felt that

even as you get older, underground spaces are exciting.

It was fun.

For lunch I tried a traditional sweet:

“Cannolo”

A crispy tube filled with sweet ricotta cream,

topped with nuts like pistachios.

Cannoli is an Italian dessert and very popular.

Personally, it wasn’t that delicious. Lol

After eating, I explored Rabat’s streets,

walked back through Mdina one more time,

and took the bus home.

🔻Connecting through fate

In the evening, I had dinner with two Japanese people working in Malta.

By the way, it was my first time meeting them.

The connection was: in May in Hakone,

there were two events on consecutive days.

One was FOMUS’s 3rd anniversary event, which I hosted.

The second was a business-owner event hosted by King Kong Nishino.

There I met Matsushima, and his niece lives in Malta,

so he connected us.

I’m so grateful for this kind of connection.

I messaged them right away,

connected, adjusted schedules,

and met today.

They work at a company in Malta,

get paid in euros,

and have a remote-work lifestyle,

which they said is very good compared to Japan’s work style.

Also, the Japanese community in Malta isn’t big,

and connections are limited.

There are few people in their late 20s to 30s,

mostly language students and business owners who have moved up.

In a way, it seems Malta is a relocation candidate for business owners in their 40s and above,

so if you can get into the community well, there may be possibilities.

Also, Malta has lots of sea,

so tuna is about 5 euros per 100g.

That price would be impossible in Dublin.

The climate is basically summer.

And there are few Japanese companies,

so in a way, many companies want people who can speak Japanese.

Also, for wedding photo shoots,

Malta is used often because the weather is good.

Indeed, I never saw wedding photos in Dublin. Lol

*Basically cloudy

So as an overseas photographer,

including Malta as an option seems good.

From Dublin, it’s about 4 hours,

so it’s not that different from Tokyo → Kyoto. Lol

So,

I learned a lot about life in Malta

and local info.

I also shared my activities a bit.

Selling masu in Malta seems difficult,

but there are places that sell alcohol,

so once I get results in Dublin,

I might approach companies in other countries too.

Another interesting thing was:

they often meet Nishino’s salon members.

Japanese business owners around 50

visit Malta as a potential relocation place,

and Matsushima connects them with Natsuki.

So they thought someone around 50 would come this time. Lol

I’m not looking for relocation,

and I don’t have excess money,

so I’m very different from them, but

people in their 20s like me seem rare,

so in a way, it’s special.

Good things happen when you move!

We had dinner at a Mediterranean restaurant.

Everything was delicious!

🔻The night continues

After dinner, we split up,

and I went to a Malta international meetup.

Tuesday is a drinking event,

and I met my Ukrainian friend there too.

I arrived late,

so I couldn’texchange with many people,

but I enjoyed the atmosphere.

A Chinese older man talked to me,

and somehow we started talking about BABYMETAL.

He really liked them and watched their videos often.

I’ve been to their live shows,

so his excitement went through the roof.

It’s rare for someone who’s seen BABYMETAL live

to show up in Malta, after all. Lol

But I like BABYMETAL though I haven’t kept up recently,

so I wanted to talk with others and end the conversation,

but cutting off someone who keeps talking is hard. Lol

I realized I still need more practice. Lol

So,

day 3 in Malta was another very interesting day!

title_en: Gozo Island Research & a Home Party
excerpt_en: Malta day 4. I had a free day, so I went solo to Gozo, the second-largest island. From Sliema, it took about 1 hour 10 minutes by bus to the ferry. I bought a ticket on the way out (the gate was open) and reached Gozo in about 30 minutes. I visited the Gozo Cathedral area and the Citadel, a huge fortress built to protect residents from pirates, and shot with the Masu turtle character. Then I had focaccia (6 euros) and gelato (two flavors for about 4 euros). I changed plans and visited the Ggantija Temples, one of the world’s oldest freestanding structures, and a useful reference for KUKU’s world. I walked about 40 minutes to a church, sat through a bishop’s talk, and reflected on how strong religion is for communities. Getting back to the ferry was a hassle because buses were full, so I walked again. That night, my host held a home party with Japanese students; we talked and played games until around 3 AM.
body_en:
Malta day 4.

I had the whole day free,

so I went to Gozo, the second-largest island in Malta.

I wanted a model to come, but

couldn’t find anyone who would go all the way to Gozo. Lol

So I went alone.

I’m staying in Sliema, so

first I took about 1 hour 10 minutes by bus to the ferry port.

I heard you can pay for the ferry on the way back,

but I bought a ticket on the way out.

It’s true that the gate was open and there was no check,

so I probably didn’t have to buy it then.

Then I took the ferry to Gozo.

The Sagishima art gallery we plan to open next year

can only be accessed by ferry too,

so in that sense it felt similar.

Gozo is huge though,

so it can’t really be compared to Sagishima. Lol

Still, traveling by ferry is exciting anywhere.

It took about 30 minutes to arrive.

I went to the Gozo Cathedral and the surrounding fortress, the “Citadel.”

It was built as a refuge to protect residents from pirate raids.

It was a massive fortress.

Here I shot the FOMUS masu character, “Masu Turtle.”

I tried not to get in people’s way while shooting,

but Masu Turtle is popular across countries,

so lots of people talked to me.

From others’ perspective, shooting Masu Turtle is unusual,

so it makes sense they’d ask. Lol

After the walk, I went to lunch.

I ate focaccia.

6 euros!

Then gelato,

two flavors for about 4 euros.

I chose chocolate and pistachio.

It wasn’t insanely good, but sweets are always nice!!

From there I planned to take a bus to a church,

but while researching I found a huge archaeological site,

so I changed the schedule immediately.

🔻To the Ggantija Temples

I went to the Ggantija Temples.

The Ggantija Temples are ancient megalithic temples on Gozo.

They were built in the Neolithic era around 3600–2500 BC,

and are among the world’s oldest freestanding structures.

The name “Ggantija” means “giant” in Maltese,

and legend says giants built them.

Features and structure

The complex consists of two adjoining temples made of massive stone blocks.

They include intricate chambers and altars,

and are believed to have been used for religious rituals.

Some stones are several meters tall.

Purpose

They were likely used for rituals related to harvest, nature, fertility, and ancestor worship.

Altars, animal bones, and pottery fragments have been found there.

World Heritage

The site was registered as a UNESCO World Heritage site in 1980,

as part of Malta’s megalithic temples.

It’s an important site for archaeology and tourism,

and lets you feel the mystery of ancient Malta.

That’s the summary.

The FOMUS character KUKU’s world “Tree Village”

also includes ruins, so

being able to see ruins that don’t exist in Japan

was valuable.

It’s far more powerful in person than in photos.

🔻A surprise 1-hour walk

After leaving the ruins,

I moved toward the church.

It was about a 40-minute walk.

I wanted to take a bus, but

there wasn’t one at a convenient time,

so I walked.

And then the church.

By chance, there was a bishop’s talk going on.

Of course I didn’t understand,

but there were many people,

and I once again felt how strong religion is as a bond.

For FOMUS,

to build a strong brand and community,

it might need to become a kind of “religion,”

and I thought more about that design.

Then, to get back to the Gozo ferry,

the buses I wanted were all packed,

and they wouldn’t even stop!

I walked about 30 minutes,

then finally caught a bus to the ferry port.

If I had kept walking, it would have taken about an hour.

On the way back, there was an actual gate

and ticket check for the ferry.

Since I had bought my ticket on the way out,

I boarded smoothly.

Then back to Malta,

and another 1-hour bus ride home.

You’d think it’s over, but

now a home party starts!

🔻Japanese home party

When I got back,

there were about three Japanese students at his house.

Today was his home party day, inviting Japanese people he met.

The cooking had already started,

and I joined midway.

I was tired from Gozo, but

that didn’t matter — I kept going.

Some were here to study after graduating,

others were still university students.

We talked and played games,

and the party went on until around 3 AM.

I haven’t joined a gathering like this recently,

so it reminded me how good home parties are.

If I could live like this in Ireland, that would be great.

I need to start looking for my next home for December. Lol

excerpt_en: After drinking late, I had a Masu Photo shoot at 11. The model was Nina, a Russian who used to live in Hokkaido; she speaks fluent Japanese. She used to live in the U.S. with her American husband, and now lives in Malta. We focused on conversation and she taught me about business in Malta. Japanese-speaking talent is rare, and “English x business x Malta” seems very promising compared to other European countries. This kind of real info only comes from meeting locals in person. At night I went to Malta’s international exchange event (6 euros with a beer). It’s a great place to practice introductions and when to bring out masu. I met many people, though it’s hard to find perfect matches. For now I just keep showing up and improving myself.
body_en:
After drinking late yesterday,

today was a shoot day.

I had a Masu Photo shoot scheduled.

The shoot was at 11.

I had set the meeting place three minutes from the house,

so moving was easy.

The model who came was Nina.

She’s Russian, but

she used to live in Hokkaido for a long time.

She has an American husband,

and they lived in the U.S.,

but now they live in Malta.

She speaks fluent Japanese, of course.

The shoot could have ended in about 30 minutes,

so we focused on conversation.

I never expected to meet a Russian who speaks fluent Japanese in Malta,

so it was really interesting!

She also told me various things about business in Malta.

Apparently Japanese-speaking talent is very rare,

and “English x business x Malta”

has far more potential than other parts of Europe,

which I realized this week.

These are things you’d never learn while staying in Japan,

and you can only learn by meeting locals in person.

This is also a strength of Masu Photo —

I can talk deeply with each person,

so it’s overwhelmingly strong.

FOMUS’s Masu Photo shoots

deepen connections

and let me learn real local information,

so it’s a truly good experience for me.

These connections happen because

I actually go there,

and because FOMUS runs ads,

which costs money, time, and energy —

but it creates even more encounters and connections.

🔻Night: international exchange event

At night, I went to Malta’s international exchange event.

I’ve spent most nights of this Malta stay

at this event.

The fee is 6 euros and includes a beer,

so it’s reasonable.

People there are studying English

or want internationalexchange,

so it’s great for making friends.

Having a place like this is very valuable.

Building strong connections is hard, but

How do I get people to know me?

I can keep trying that here,

so it’s a great learning environment.

How to introduce myself to people overseas,

and when to bring out the masu —

being able to keep doing trial and error

is the best.

*Not many people use it this way

Again, I met people from many countries.

Of course, it’s hard to connect with someone who really clicks,

and it doesn’t happen easily, but

if I don’t step up to the plate,

that chance won’t come at all,

so for now I just keep showing up

and meeting people nonstop.

I’ll keep polishing myself

so I can connect with people doing even more interesting things.

excerpt_en: Today I shot in Malta under rain. Two shoots were scheduled; the first canceled same-day saying it was cold and they’d catch a cold, which felt disrespectful, so I focused on the second. She was a Maltese model studying to be a tour guide, and her English was very clear. We shot nearby due to the rain. Afterward one of the Masu accessories had fallen off; I retraced my steps and found it. Then I explored Valletta, ate McDonald’s, and re-tried a gelato shop for pistachio and hazelnut — much better than the first time. I watched the Valletta noon gun and went home. At night I went to another meetup alone, met mostly students and people over 40, and later took a walk with Mikola. He’s traveling to Vienna for work next week. I realize my English improves the most when I’m with him.
body_en:
Today I shot in Malta again.

But it was raining.

There were two shoots scheduled,

but the first was a last-minute cancellation.

Honestly, I didn’t think she was someone I really wanted to shoot,

so I didn’t mind the cancellation, but

the way she canceled — on the day of —

“It’s cold so I’ll catch a cold if I shoot, so I won’t.”

That felt disrespectful, to be honest.

Still, it’s a waste to spend energy on someone who won’t come,

so I shot with the other person.

By coincidence, the second person wanted the first person’s time slot,

so the schedule moved smoothly with no waiting.

She was a pure Maltese model,

and her English was very easy to understand.

She’s studying to be a tour guide,

so that explains it! Lol

Since it was raining,

we kept the shoot close by.

But after the shoot, something happened.

One of the Masu accessories had fallen off her ear.

Of course I knew it wasn’t intentional,

so after the shoot I went to look for it.

We hadn’t walked far,

so I retraced our path,

and it was there!!!

Huge relief. Lol

This was the first time an accessory went missing.

These kinds of incidents happen sometimes,

so I try to stay calm and judge properly.

If I get shaken by this,

I can’t do business.

So,

the Masu Photo shoot went fine!

🔻Occasional “waste” spending

After the shoot,

I refueled.

I could have gone straight home, but

since I was already in Valletta,

I explored the city despite the rain.

I skipped all tourist spots,

since I visited them in my first Valletta trip.

The cathedral was extremely crowded.

For lunch, there was McDonald’s,

so I kept it simple.

After that,

I went to the gelato shop I skipped last time

because the pistachio was sold out.

Retry!

Today it was in stock!

So I chose

pistachio and hazelnut.

They’re both nuts, but

I love both, so no problem!

The pistachio was 100x better than the first gelato I had!

I was surprised how much gelato flavor changes by shop.

Then at 16:00,

I watched the Valletta cannon that marks time,

and went home.

🔻Night meetup

At night, I went to Malta’s exchange meetup again.

In this case, Mikola (who I live with)

had other plans, so I went alone.

It’s important that as many people as possible

know me and FOMUS,

and it improves my English communication,

so there’s no option to skip.

There are a lot of students and people over 40 here.

Their English levels are sometimes about the same as mine,

and sometimes lower or higher.

Even with lower English,

high communication skills can cover it,

so you see people like that too.

In the end, I enjoyed the first couple of hours,

and then left because I didn’t find anyone I wanted to talk to.

When I got home, Mikola returned around the same time and said, “Let’s walk,”

so we walked for about 30 minutes.

Since I’m always communicating in English,

I feel my English improves the most when I’m with Mikola.

He told me he suddenly has to go to Vienna next week for work.

He’s also an entrepreneur,

so he travels for business often,

and since he’s invited, the client covers the travel costs.

I want to get to that position too,

and discovering that such work exists was fascinating.

These experiences really require local connections.

So much to learn.

After all that, I went to sleep after midnight.

By the way,

my talks with Mikola are business or romance.

He likes romance topics, and also

because I’m so focused on business,

so we talk about other things too. Lol

Of course, he’s letting me stay,

so I go along with it. Lol
